title: Part 2
text: (2004) diagnosed as gnostic, to the extent that human beings ‘artificially’ sought to create order out of disorder through their own ordering devices, claiming participation to be some ‘mystery of being’, or some ‘primary source of truth’. Voegelin also argued that this amounted to an intellectual hubris that was deeply nihilistic in nature, emptying the world of meaning exactly as it was searched for. Nietzsche had of course come up with a very similar diagnosis of the modern world and its world-denying attitude – that attitude which Weber sociologized via his central organizing concept of ‘the religious rejections of the Into Liminality 7 World’ and their ‘directions’ (Weber 1948). But in contrast to Nietzsche, Voegelin understood that any return to ‘pure experience’ would just exacerbate the problem as identified. Kierkegaard had come to much the same conclusion a century before. By situating liminality as a central concept for social theory, one must therefore also be conscious of its limits – and limits matter! So let us state it plainly: liminality does not and cannot ‘explain’ anything. Liminality cannot replace any other term that forms part of causal explanatory frameworks. In liminality there is no certainty concerning the outcome. At its broadest, liminality refers to any ‘betwixt and between’ situation or object, any in-between place or moment, a state of suspense, a moment of freedom between two structured world-views or institutional arrangements. It relates to change in a single personality as well as social change and transition in large-scale settings; it therefore also, and perhaps more directly so than any other concept we have, ties together the micro and the macro, operating from the ‘middle’. Liminality opens the door to a world of contingency where events and meanings – indeed ‘reality’ itself – can be moulded and carried in different directions. Liminality explains nothing. Liminality is. It happens. It takes place. And human beings react to liminal experiences in different ways. Those ways cannot be easily predicted. But they can be analysed and compared, and at the formal level they share important properties. And this is what this book sets out to discuss, with reference to a variety of liminal situations, in a variety of societies and historical settings. Briefly put, the concept of liminality can help us understand transition periods and social processes of change in a different light. Liminality: Beyond Good and Evil Liminality has in recent years been taken up in a myriad of ways. It would be utterly impossible and probably also futile to undertake any comprehensive literature review of current usages of the term, not least because one would need to stretch such a discussion across at least twenty disciplines, ranging from religious studies and anthropology to marketing and consulting. While liminality is certainly not a term that one would ever wish to ‘appropriate’ by prividing clear-cut definitions and boxes, the danger must be recognized that as a concept it can and easily come to signify almost anything. This book therefore invites a reflexive use of the term, rooted in its intellectual and anthropological history, and with due stress on the concepts of experience and transition. There is one widespread but highly problematic usage of the concept that one can and must signal from the outset, namely the tendency to use liminality as synomymous with ‘marginality’: to simply posit ‘liminal subjects’ as those who are marginalized and socially excluded. There is an extent to which liminality in recent years has invaded our academic (and popular) vocabularies as part of a fashion, identifying ever new forms of social exclusion and renaming existing ones. Used in such a way, the term has nothing additional to offer. While liminality and marginality share affinities (being boundary-concepts), they are also very Liminality and the Modern 8 different terms: that which is interstitial is neither marginal nor on the outside; liminality refers, quite literally, to something placed in an in-between position. For the very same reasons, ‘liminality’ is not something simply to be celebrated or wished for. Quite the contrary: liminality needs to be duly and carefully problematized, and this is particularly important in a period such as ours which instinctively adopts a celebratory attitude towards anything that represents novelty and constant ‘innovation’, this mantra which haunts us more than ever and permeates our economy, our politics, our work, our life-conduct, our governmentalities. Any serious company or institution today evidently needs an innovation strategy, and when it does not function, then the innovation strategy must be innovated. This boundary-breaking attitude is in essence the very hallmark of modernity, or what Bauman (2000) called ‘liquid modernity’, but it is imploding and ‘stabilizing’ at the core of our social existence in ways that we still need to understand. In a way, that is the overall aim of this book, although it evidently cannot be but a very small, first step towards such a task. Part of the reason why liminality has unproblematically come to connote something ‘positive’ has to do with intellectual developments since the 1980s. One pertinent example can be traced in the reception and current popularity of Mikhail Bakhtin’s indeed very perceptive discussion of the forces of laughter, carnival and the ‘carnivalesque’ as these transform in modern society (Bakhtin 1984). The problematic celebration of the carnivalesque may be detected in Bakhtin’s original work, as when he notes how in carnival the ‘utopian and the realistic merged’ (ibid.: 10), creating a social situation of pure and immediate human relations void of hierarchy. But the way in which Bakhtin’s work came to be seen as a precursor to a postmodern ethos liberated from the fixity of structures, a genuine ‘destruction as create passion’ (to use Bakunin’s famed expression), is hugely problematic, and fails to incorporate all the warning signs signalled by Bakhtin himself. To write from the interstices, from the in-between, also turned into an analytical strategy in postmodern and postcolonial literature. For Homi Bhabha (1994), liminality was quite simply a positive expression of cultural hybridity. This position has been adopted within cultural studies and anthropology alike: liminality has come to stand for a cultural hybridity that entertains difference without an assumed or imposed hierarchy. In anthropology such a position of hybridity was embraced in an attitude of distance-taking towards ‘classical anthropology’ which had – allegedly – fixed persons and cultures in essentialized time-space units, reifying the world before us. Liminality, with one stroke of magic, undoes all that, dissolving any fixity of position, dissolving the modern into permanent hybridity. In recent years, this notion of liminality as creative hybridity has entered the wider vocabularies of the political sciences. As we have discussed elsewhere in the context of the multiple modernities ‘paradigm’ (Thomassen 2012c), to pluralize and ‘write into history’ hitherto excluded subjects by discovering ‘hybrid’ or ‘alternative modernities’ has by both writers and social theorists been employed as a means of resistance to the new global (neo-liberal) order, and a call for new forms of justice and inclusion. Into Liminality 9 But at the heart of our celebratory attitudes toward pluralities of the modern lies a theoretical misconception that needs to be spelled out with some clarity. This misconception rests on the conclusion reached in the 1980s, namely that ‘old’ modernity represented a uniformizing/rationalizing force. It is against this background that plural modernities, liminality and hybridity of forms become both ‘good’ and necessary. It is certainly true that in the social sciences the dominance of modernization theories in the post-war period led to a view of modernity as uniform across time and space, equating it with Western ideals, ideas and institutions. The problem is that this idea of modernity as unitary and uniformizing was always wrong. It is even conceptually wrong. The very notion of being modern was, from its inception in the early sixteenth century precisely not about creating ‘sameness’, as it indeed demarcated a difference from the past (the ancients), and a notion of continuously evolving horizons. The modern was the new, the diverse, the forward-looking: it had no definite form and no definite end result, other than a continuously moving horizon; it was about change and movement, speeding up, dissolving and transforming everything at hand (Koselleck 1979; Wagner 2008; Giesen 2009). That is also why we hesitate to adopt Bauman’s view that modernity was once ‘hard’, before it eventually became liquid (in ‘postmodernity’). It is rather the case that the ‘hardness’ of modernity was from its inception built upon and tied to its seeming opposite, as a co-constituting element rather than an alternative: the out- of-the-ordinary, the transgressive, the liminal. Victor Turner himself played a huge role in this positioning of hybridity and liminality as emancipatory tool. One of his most read essays ever, The Anthropology of Performance (1988), opens with a dream-like wish: ‘For years I have dreamed of a liberated anthropology’. Turner came to see ‘post-modernism’ as the key to that liberation, and liminality the one central concept to unfold within that vision. One part of Turner’s assessment of the social sciences is difficult to disagree with. Anthropology embedded in the modern worldview, from evolutionism to functionalism and structuralism, involved ‘a systematic dehumanizing of the human subjects of study, regarding them as bearers of an impersonal “culture” [...], determined by social, cultural or social psychological “forces”, “variables”, or “pressures”’ (ibid.: 72). This modern, scientific worldview now belongs to the past, says Turner (ibid.: 72). Instead we have a more open discourse with a new blending of genres and disciplinary boundary crossings. The obsessions with ‘fit and congruence’ (ibid.: 74) is replaced with Turner’s own view of the field as a ‘set of loosely integrated processes, with some patterned aspects, some persistences of form, but controlled by discrepant principles of action expressed in rules of custom that are often situationally incompatible with one another’ (ibid.). Turner’s position here is a neat expression of the view, established since the 1980s, that postmodernism involved a liberation of genres and a going beyond the rationalizing and confining tendency of the modern world-view. This postmodern turn, says Turner, ‘involves the processualization of space, its temporalization, as against the spatialization of process or time, which we found to be the essence Liminality and the Modern 10 of the modern’ (ibid.: 76). As we shall discuss further, Turner placed this view in the method called ‘social drama analysis’, linking it to a broader paradigmatic shift towards a processual social science which sees social interaction itself as theatrical and staged in Goffman’s sense; a kind of social science that opens up the field toward the performative genres, including literature, theatre and arts. Social dramas are here defined by Turner as ‘units of aharmonic or disharmonic social process, arising in conflict situations’ (ibid.). And it is this process that Turner engaged, equipped with the tripartite structure of van Gennep’s ritual passage. In so far as this paves the ground for Turner’s incredibly useful analysis of social dramas in their unfolding, this is all well-taken and hugely important. However, Turner’s own analysis of the kind of ‘liberty’ that explodes in liminality should perhaps have made him more reflexive with regard to the generative powers of liminality. When all is said and done, Turner preferred to see liminality as a fascinating and necessary shaking of routines, a cultural space of human creativity. As we shall discuss more in detail, it was in this vein that Turner coined the term ‘liminoid’ to denote how liminal practices re-emerged in modern consumer societies via art, theatre and the larger leisure culture industry. This again involves a celebratory stance towards the non-systemic and towards creativity, play and fantasy. However, here Turner simplifies matters, and we shall argue that his cultural analysis needs a different kind of sociological rooting. Moreover, much like Homi Bhabha, he translates an analytical position into a normative ideal. Turner’s take on modernity is also misleading in a very fundamental way, and this is because the modern was never just about rationalizing, measuring and categorizing: it was in a quite fundamental sense equally about ‘play and fantasy’, and it was, in particular, about how such indeed liminal fantasies and carnivalesque impulses became genealogically tied to rationalization processes (Szakolczai 2013a). As Shakespeare intuited so famously, within a horizon of modernity all the world has become one stage. Hence, by celebrating the liminal as a going beyond modernity, we have indeed been celebrating the very most problematic aspect of the modern, and we have failed to come to grips with some of its underlying mechanisms. The problem with this identification of the liminal as a sheer, liberating force can best be grasped by reference to Plato’s ontology, presented in his late writing, Philebus. Plato divides all that exists (23C) into four classes: the limited, the unlimited, their combination, and the causes of their combination. The rest of the dialogue is largely concerned with arguing for the superiority of the third, mixed class over the first two ‘pure’ cases (while saying little about causes, left for the follow-up dialogue, Timaeus). In other words, if the modern Kantian worldview was obsessed with the limits of thought, constraining all human experience into locked and ultimately empty categories, then postmodern thinking jumped into the other abyss, celebrating anything that was without limits. On this note, Plato would not have hesitated to place postmoderns alongside the Sophists, who constantly showed ignorance of the need for limits; as he would have had little patience with those ‘rational thinkers’ who do not recognize the centrality of Into Liminality 11 limit-experiences. To think with liminality very basically means to realize that human life is organized as a precarious balance between the limit and the limitless. ‘Freedom’ is an empty word outside such a balance of existence, or, as Voegelin called it, a ‘balance of consciousness’. The Guiding Idea of this Book: The Implosion of Liminality in Modernity The contemporary scene seems to be characterized by an increasingly ambivalent attitude toward liminality: on the one hand a fear of liminal experiences as truly personality transforming events, on the other hand a celebratory stance towards any kind of liminality. On the one hand, and as stressed very much by Foucault (1979), the modern world was always characterized by closing off everything that lay beyond the boundaries of rationality. This hallmark of modernity as excluding such ‘limit’ experiences was perhaps expressed most clearly by Kant. On the other hand it has also been argued, and by a series of thinkers, writers and artists, that the modern world is somehow a ‘carnival’, a grotesque, never-ending comedy, in which ‘limit-experiences’ turn into norm, a frenzy that never really cools down. An underlying question permeating this book is this: How are we to understand this mixture of rationality and comedy? How, and in what ways, have ‘social dramas’ and liminal experiences become part of the modern figuration? The guiding idea behind the writing of this book can be expressed in simple terms: it is the idea that liminality somehow came to occupy a more and more central place within the space of modernity, a process which is currently accelerating to the point of absurdity. There are two larger dimensions to this process, spatial and temporal. Modernity as the Centralization of Liminality Having essentially to do with an in-between position, the point of departure for any analysis of liminality must be spatial. Not surprisingly, van Gennep’s book from 1909 contains a crucial, full chapter on ‘the territorial passage’, the title of Chapter 2 which followed immediately upon his initial classification of rites. In this chapter, van Gennep describes this situation in Europe before the demarcation of national boundaries: ‘Each country was surrounded by a strip of neutral ground which in practice was divided into sections or marches [...] Zones of this kind were important in classical antiquity, especially in Greece, where they were used for market places or battlefields’ (1960: 17–18). The two examples invoked here by van Gennep are absolutely fundamental, as they touch upon the origins of the two institutions that would come to give shape to European modernity: market economy and warfare. Both activities, warfare and trade, were in most cultural contexts of which we have record, carried out in liminal spatial figurations, in what van Gennep also calls ‘neutral zones’. Such zones were in-between areas, standing outside the normal social order, and therefore required Liminality and the Modern 12 a cultural elaboration, a ritual passage. This also meant that activities taking place here could under no circumstance be carried into the ordinary life of the surrounding settlements, as this would undermine the stability and very texture of social life. This is, of course, in a nutshell, what later happened, and with effects on meaningful social relations that we still need to decipher. Entertainment industries thriving on sex, comedy and violence now operate from the very spatial centres of cities, in what Simmel fittingly called the ‘economic jungle of the Metropolis’. The empty space of borderlands has settled within the neutral spaces of Metropolitan shopping areas, where strangers mingle freely. Never before in the history of the world have non-places occupied so much space (Benko and Strohmayer 1997: 23; Augé 1995). Meanwhile, warfare has itself become permanentized in both space and time (Mallksö 2012). Surprisingly enough, this danger was signaled with great clarity by Plato (1995), in his discussion of the khora in Timaeus. The question relates to one of Plato’s main concerns in his late writings, namely the productive powers of nothingness, and how non-being turns into being. Here Plato reluctantly had to go beyond Parmenides, who had always insisted that nothing can grow out of nothing, and that one should therefore not talk about nothingness. But evidently Plato sensed that the end of the classical world critically had to do with exactly such a proliferation of non-space (in which the Sophist thrives). Khora is a void, an abyss, in which things can reproduce themselves infinitely, it ‘re-flects’ like a mirror that is not affected by the image it reflects. Khora is without limit, without bottom, without ground. The Khora is nothingness and infinity at the same time. Plato, the founder of Western epistemology, somehow saw this implosion of nothingness as a lurking threat to the ordered world around him. But in that recognition Plato makes himself a theorist of the contemporary. It is this historical development that one can attempt to capture, using van Gennep’s framework of analysis; and in spatial terms the process can best be described as a centralization of liminality. In fact, the very passing from one zone to the other has in different cultural contexts been accompanied by specific rituals, as moving in and out of such spaces could be both dangerous and contaminating, so therefore had to be controlled, constrained and ritualized. In many cases, one would need a special permit to pass though a neutral zone. In Europe, as discussed by van Gennep, this permit was called a ‘letter of marque’. Letters of marque originally constituted a license from a sovereign authorizing a subject to seek reprisals against subjects of a hostile state. It is from these observations that van Gennep can pinpoint the larger purpose of his book, Rites of Passage, and to some extent of his entire academic project: Whoever passes from one [zone] to the other finds himself physically and magico-religiously in a special situation for a certain length of time: he wavers between two worlds. It is this situation which I have designated a transition, and one of the purposes of this book is to demonstrate that this symbolic and spatial area of transition may be found in more or less pronounced form in all of the Into Liminality 13 ceremonies which accompany the passage from one social and magico-religious position to another. (van Gennep 1960: 18) From this re-statement of purpose, van Gennep moves on with rich descriptions of territorial passages in a variety of cultural contexts, starting with ancient Greece, and with the emblematic example of rituals carried out when a Spartan king went to war. Van Gennep then stresses that such procedures apply not only with reference to countries, but also in reference to a village, a town, a section of a town, a temple, or a house: ‘The neutral zone shrinks progressively till it ceases to exist except as a simple stone, a beam, or a threshold’ (ibid.: 19). The sacrality of space is written into these figurations, and rests upon a very basic recognition of boundaries, of division of spaces, of taboos surrounding and protecting the sacrality of places and boundaries, and of the necessary existence of in-between spaces or neutral zones that in all cultures are duly ritualized and venerated. Hence the near-universality of rituals pertaining to the door. The door is the boundary between the foreign and domestic worlds in the case of an ordinary dwelling, between the profane and sacred worlds in the case of a temple (ibid.: 20). Janus, the two-faced Roman god of warfare, was also deity of doors and thresholds. To cross the threshold is to unite oneself with another world. As simple as it may sound, meaningful human experience of social environments is simply not possible without such discernment. Boundaries are necessary for the framing of human experience and for thought itself. We learn to relate to the world like this; and this was captured very well by Bachelard (1994) in his Poetics of Space. Passing through a door – how irrelevantly banal! For van Gennep that one concrete act comes to stand as a condensed expression of human life itself. Doors are the passage-ways. Rites carried out on the threshold, says van Gennep, are transition rites. Rituals of purification (cleansing, washing) are rites of separation from previous surroundings; then follows rites of incorporation (often in the form of a shared meal): ‘The rites of the threshold are therefore not ‘union’ ceremonies, properly speaking, but rites of preparation for union, themselves preceded by rites of preparation for the transitional stage’. And here follows van Gennep’s refinement of his initial distinction of the three-dimensional nature of ritual passages: ‘I propose to call the rites of separation from a previous world, preliminal rites, those executed during the transitional stage liminal (or threshold) rites, and the ceremonies of incorporation into the new world post-liminal rites’. (1960: 23). Hence, to think with liminality very basically has to do with a thorough understanding of passages and passage experiences. Rooted in the Latin patio (to suffer, to undergo), the word ‘passage’ indicates a displacement, a process of transformation undertaken, but not yet finished. It designates equally ‘the site where this process, its mark or its support are enacted whatever its morphological or metaphorical sense’ (La Soudière 2000: 5). Hence, a passage is at its heart Liminality and the Modern 14 related to a certain kind of experience that one is undergoing, a movement from one position to another. Permanent Liminality: the Temporal Fixation of Liminal Conditions The spatial ‘stretching’ of liminality and the centralization of liminality here hinted to is accompanied by a temporal process, whereby liminality – in some cases, and under certain circumstances – takes on a more permanent form. Arpad Szakolczai (2000) has in this regard famously diagnosed modernity itself as a particular form of ‘permanent liminality’. In many ways, this book is an attempt to discuss, elaborate and exemplify this process of ‘permanentization’, standing in close proximity to what Weber termed ‘routinization’. The central point here seems to be the following: with regard to out-of-limit experiences, far from evaporating and being pushed to the margins, as assumed both by modernist theories of evolution, and by postmodern celebrations of the playful as an antidote to instrumentalist reason, something very different happened from the sixteenth century onwards: liminality became established at the core of the modern project. Play, comedy, gambling, sexuality, entertainment, violence – in short, all the most evident aspects of liminality linked to human experience – took central stage within cultural, political and economic modernity. Simultaneously, at the level of thought, the human sentiments of fear, anxiety, scepticism and doubt (quintessential liminal sentiments) were established as anthropological foundations. This is the overall claim that this book wishes to pursue, most directly in Part II, tracing this centralization and permanentization of liminality in a variety of directions, referring first and foremost to cultural and political developments. Chapter Structure This book is divided into two parts. Each part is composed of four chapters that form a whole. Part I is introductory and constitutes an attempt to situate Arnold van Gennep and the concept of liminity within its proper historical and disciplinary context. It is an engagement with intellectual history, but for a purpose. This part provides a proper and thorough introduction to the concept of liminality and discusses how it developed within anthropology in the early twentieth century, and later within the social and human sciences from the 1960s until today. It thus seeks to establish a more solid ground for applying liminality as an analytical tool of relevance for the social sciences writ large. Any book on liminality must depart from anthropology: it was via anthropology that liminality entered our conceptual vocabulary, and it could not be otherwise. Chapters 1 and 2 of this book will set the frame by discussing how the concept of liminality emerged and developed via the work of Arnold van Gennep. While it is well-known that Arnold van Gennep came up with the concept of liminality Into Liminality 15 in his book Rites of Passage, subsequent scholarship has completely bypassed how this discovery was part of a larger intellectual project that van Gennep was trying to establish in the early twentieth century. This is the focus of Chapter 1. Before van Gennep eventually turned to the exclusive study of folklore (and this happened in the early 1920s), this academic project developed in sometimes open competition with Emile Durkheim. Chapter 2 will discuss this largely unknown intellectual battle and explain why van Gennep’s project regrettably drowned in oblivion while Durkheim’s functionalist approach largely triumphed. Sociologists and anthropologists with a specific interest in intellectual history can read this chapter on its own. Chapter 3 will proceed to describe how the concept of liminality re-emerged in the 1960s, initially via the writings of Victor Turner. The chapter will present Turner’s development of liminality, indicating how liminality must be tied to an experientially based process-approach. However, the chapter will end on a series of warning notes already alluded to: namely that we should not simply follow Turner’s suggestion that liminality in our modern world has turned into the ‘liminoid’. This is certainly the suggestion that made Victor Turner famous well beyond anthropology, and made him a reference figure in a diversity of study areas, from theatre to literature to tourism studies. We argue instead that any application of liminality must, as a minimum, stay close to one aspect of its original meaning as defined by van Gennep: namely, that is has to do with the passing of a threshold and therefore with transition. If it is not about transition, it is not about liminality. If adopting Turner’s notion of the liminoid in the analysis of liminal-like phenomena in modern, consumerist society does not represent a convincing analytical strategy, Chapter 4 suggests ways in which the concept of liminality can indeed serve to highlight dynamics of thought, experience and social change in both ‘simple’ and less simple social situations. This involves a careful consideration of how a concept derived from ritual passages in small-scale societies can be applied to social and political change in larger social complexes, or even entire civilizational settings. It is suggested that Turner’s study of pilgrimage may in fact represent a more promising development. Here Turner indicated how a liminal state may become ‘fixed’, referring to a situation in which the suspended character of social life takes on a more permanent character. This was much in line with what Turner himself had suggested earlier, namely that in the monastic and mendicant states of the world religions, transition had become a permanent condition. This point – again, the ‘permanentization’ of liminal conditions – will be taken up in subsequent chapters. Chapter 4 thus stands midway between Parts I and II, seeking to reconstruct and redirect Victor Turner’s project, also referring back to some of Turner’s less discussed last writings. The central argument proposed in this chapter, drawing here extensively on the analysis offered by Arpad Szakolczai, is also that Turner’s recognition of the proximity of his own project with that of the German philosopher Dilthey was correct and important. The chapter will discuss the implications of that statement, and those implications will serve to identify a framework for thinking with liminality which relates to transition and change Liminality and the Modern 16 both at the individual and societal levels. Concerning the connections between the experience of liminality and modern thought, the impact of the Lisbon earthquake on Kant’s thinking will be invoked as a particularly salient example. The chapters that make up Part II are in different ways attempts to apply this framework to specific practices or historical situations. They each engage a particular aspect of liminality within a horizon of modernity. Chapter 5 addresses the larger time period that followed the ‘waning of the Middle Ages’ (Huizinga) and the collapse of the Renaissance to the mid seventeenth century, the period often referred to as ‘early modernity’. This historical period, following here Huizinga and Voegelin, was not experienced by contemporaries as a ‘breakthrough’ or a passage toward a more mature modernity, but rather as a total collapse of order, an age of anxiety, an ‘age of great confusion’. The age was in desperate search for new ordering principles within politics, philosophy and science. Two cases will briefly be invoked: the life experiences and related thought systems of the contemporaries Thomas Hobbes and René Descartes. It will be argued that the disorder of the early modern age was overcome with the ideational and political-institutional structure that was established with the ‘moment of Westphalia’, and with Cartesian science laying the foundations for
